elements.sent-2411-001: We propose a scheme for constructing versatile quantum simulators using ultracold Rydberg atoms in long-lived circular and elliptical states.
elements.sent-2411-002: By exciting different subspaces of internal atomic states, the atoms can be used to simulate two effective spin species with different spin-spin interactions.
elements.sent-2411-003: The strengths of transverse and longitudinal spin-spin interactions, both intra- and inter-species, can be controlled within a wide range of values.
elements.sent-2411-004: This setup can be used to simulate two-species spin models or lattice models with two sublattices.
elements.sent-2411-005: We show examples of specific models which can be realized.
elements.sent-2411-006: Quantum simulation allows to investigate complex physical models, with unprecedented level of control and insight into the system properties.
elements.sent-2411-007: Technological advancements now allow for the construction of quantum simulators using arrays of ultracold particles trapped in precisely positioned optical tweezers~\cite{2018-Barredo-Nature,2019-Anderegg-Science,2021-Altman-PRXQ,2022-Jenkins-PRX,2022-Bluvstein-Nature,2022-Zhang-QSciTech,2024-Gyger-PRR .}
elements.sent-2411-008: The study of spin models is a crucial application of these simulators.
elements.sent-2411-009: Spin model simulators can be realized by treating internal levels of the particles as effective ``spin'' states, with interactions between particles mapped onto effective ``spin interactions''.
elements.sent-2411-010: Previous proposals for spin model simulators include setups made up of ultracold polar molecules~\cite{2024-Cornish-NatPhys , atoms~\cite{2017-Gross-Science , or their mixtures~\cite{2023-Dobrzyniecki-PRA .}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Atoms in highly-excited Rydberg states are especially promising for quantum simulations due to their strong, tunable interactions, including complex anisotropic dipolar couplings~\cite{2010-Saffman-RevModPhys,2019-Adams-JPB .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Various studies have explored the use of Rydberg atom systems for quantum information processing~\cite{2000-Jaksch-PRL,2001-Lukin-PRL,2010-Isenhower-PRL , simulation of spin models~\cite{2010-Weimer-NatPhys,2013-Gunter-Science,2015-Barredo-PRL,2018-Schauss-QST,2018-DeLeseleuc-PRL,2020-Browaeys-NatPhys,2020-Celi-PRX,2021-Wu-CPB,2024-JuliaFarre-PRA-Quantized,2024-Mukherjee-Arxiv , and simulation of hard-core bosons in a lattice~\cite{2024-Poon-AdvQuantumTech .}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The simulators based on Rydberg atoms have possible uses for studying quantum magnetism~\cite{2024-JuliaFarre-PRA-Amorphous,2024-Homeier-Arxiv,2024-Liu-Arxiv-Supersolidity and topological phases~\cite{2019-DeLeseleuc-Science,2023-Kornjaca-CommPhys,2024-Zeng-Arxiv .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Notably, a recent paper considers a Rydberg atom array composed of two different atom species, which can exhibit more varied interactions than single-species arrays~\cite{2024-Liu-Arxiv-Novel .}
elements.sent-2411-015: In addition to theoretical proposals, Rydberg atom-based quantum simulators have seen promising experimental realizations~\cite{2015-Barredo-PRL,2016-Labuhn-Nature,2017-Bernien-Nature,2019-DeLeseleuc-Science,2020-Schlosser-JPB,2021-Semeghini-Science,2021-Scholl-Nature,2021-Ebadi-Nature,2021-Signoles-PRX,2022-Scholl-PRXQ,2023-Steinert-PRL,2024-Bornet-PRL .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Among the various Rydberg states, circular states have attracted considerable attention.
elements.sent-2411-017: Circular states have the maximum possible magnetic quantum number ($|m_\ell| = \ell = n-1$, where $\ell$, $n$ are the azimuthal and principal quantum numbers).
elements.sent-2411-018: They exhibit notably long lifetimes due to limited radiative decay pathways.
elements.sent-2411-019: The radiative lifetimes of circular states scale as $n^5$, and typical circular level lifetimes are on the order of $10^{-2}\,\mathrm{s}$ (e.g. $2.5\times10^{-2}\,\mathrm{s}$ for $n = 48$)~\cite{2018-Nguyen-PRX .}
elements.sent-2411-020: In comparison, the lifetimes of low-$\ell$ Rydberg states scale approximately as $n^3$; for $n = 48$ they are on the order of $10^{-4}\,\mathrm{s}$~\cite{1994-Gallagher-Book .}
elements.sent-2411-021: The lifetime of circular states can be further extended by placing the atoms inside small cavities that do not support the modes corresponding to their decay wavelengths~\cite{1981-Kleppner-PRL,1985-Hulet-PRL .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Techniques for preparing circular Rydberg states are well-established~\cite{1983-Hulet-PRL,1986-Liang-PRA,2018-Morgan-PRA,2020-CantatMoltrecht-PRR , with recent advancements allowing for precise positioning of these atoms in arrays~\cite{2023-Ravon-PRL,2024-Holzl-PRX .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Very recently, dipolar interactions between trapped circular atoms have been observed experimentally~\cite{2024-Mehaignerie-Arxiv .}
elements.sent-2411-024: These facts make circular Rydberg states a promising tool for quantum simulation~\cite{2023-Mehaignerie-Thesis , and the rapid advancement of experimental techniques makes circular states a timely subject.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Several proposals have explored practical applications of circular Rydberg states.
elements.sent-2411-026: For instance, some proposals have suggested using the entire ladder of Rydberg levels, from low-$\ell$ to circular levels, to simulate the states of an arbitrarily large spin~\cite{2014-Signoles-NatPhys,2022-Kruckenhauser-QST .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Recently, detailed proposals have been published for using systems of cold circular Rydberg atoms in quantum simulation~\cite{2018-Nguyen-PRX and computation~\cite{2021-Cohen-PRXQ .}}
elements.sent-2411-028: These proposals use a scheme where the two spin-1/2 states are encoded in two circular states.
elements.sent-2411-029: This approach typically yields effective spins with strong spin-exchanging couplings, in contrast to weaker longitudinal couplings~\cite{2023-Mehaignerie-Thesis .}
elements.sent-2411-030: In this paper, we investigate a different scheme, where the two spin-1/2 states are encoded as a circular level and an elliptical (i.e. $|m_\ell| = n-2$) level.
elements.sent-2411-031: This results in effective spins for which longitudinal spin-spin couplings are much stronger than spin-exchanging couplings.
elements.sent-2411-032: We evaluate and compare the interaction strengths in both encoding schemes.
elements.sent-2411-033: Furthermore, we explore the possibility of combining both schemes to simulate a ``two-species'' spin model with tunable inter- and intra-species interactions.
elements.sent-2411-034: Such a model can be simulated with an array of identical atoms, flexibly assigned to either ``spin species'' as desired.
elements.sent-2411-035: This simplifies preparation and allows the same atom arrangement to simulate systems with various species populations.
elements.sent-2411-036: We furthermore demonstrate two example systems which can be realized in this approach.
elements.sent-2411-037: We note that elliptical Rydberg states have received less attention in the literature, being treated mostly as sources of unwanted couplings of circular states.
elements.sent-2411-038: Our work finds a practical application for elliptical states, providing an initial exploration of their potential for new quantum simulation schemes.
elements.sent-2411-039: This paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-040: In Section~\ref{sec:model , we describe a physical system of ultracold atoms and outline the two distinct mapping schemes for creating effective spins.}
elements.sent-2411-041: In Section~\ref{sec:effective-interactions , we analyze the resulting effective spin interactions in these two distinct spin species.}
elements.sent-2411-042: In Section~\ref{sec:example-systems , we showcase example models which can be created with this approach.}
elements.sent-2411-043: In Section~\ref{sec:experimental , we discuss the experimental feasibility of realizing the proposed two-species setup.}
elements.sent-2411-045: We consider a system of $N$ atoms, modeled as point-like particles and numbered as $j=1,\ldots,N$.
elements.sent-2411-046: The atoms are placed at fixed locations $\vec{R _j$.}
elements.sent-2411-047: The Hamiltonian describing this system is \begin{equation}\label{eq:physical_hamiltonian} \hat{H} = \sum\limits_{j=1}^N \hat{h}^{(j)} + \sum\limits_{j < k=1}^N \hat{V}^{(j,k)},\end{equation} where $\hat{h ^{(j)}$ is the one-body Hamiltonian describing the internal levels of atom $j = 1,...,N$, and $\hat{V ^{(j,k)}$ describes the interaction between atoms $j$ and $k$.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: We assume all $N$ atoms are described by identical one-body Hamiltonians $\hat{h ^{(j)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Additionally, we assume the system is made up of alkali-metal atoms, which are a common choice in cold-atom experiments and quantum simulators due to their favorable properties.
elements.sent-2411-050: The exact choice of atomic element is inconsequential, since circular-state valence electrons feel very little interaction with the nucleus, and their properties depend only negligibly on the atomic number.
elements.sent-2411-051: The system is subjected to a uniform electric field $\vecEdc$ and a magnetic field $\vec{B}$, both aligned along the $Z$-axis (i.e., the quantization axis), of strengths $\Edc$ and $B$ respectively.
elements.sent-2411-052: The effects of $\vecEdc$ and $\vec{B}$ on the atomic levels are incorporated in $\hat{h ^{(j)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Throughout the paper, we assume the magnetic field strengths are constrained to below 1000 Gauss, as higher values are challenging to obtain in ultracold-atoms laboratory settings.
elements.sent-2411-054: Similarly, the maximum electric field strength is limited in practice, because at high $\Edc$ Rydberg atoms undergo spontaneous ionization.
elements.sent-2411-055: Note that circular states can withstand higher electric fields compared to low-$\ell$ states of the same $n$.
elements.sent-2411-056: As an example, in Ref.~\cite{1983-Hulet-PRL , the ionization thresholds for circular $n=19$ Li states were estimated as $6\,\mathrm{kV/cm}$, compared to $4.6\,\mathrm{kV/cm}$ for $m_\ell = 2$ states of same $n$.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Similarly, Ref.~\cite{2020-Teixeira-PRL gives the threshold field as $\approx 125\,\mathrm{V/cm}$ for the $n=51$ circular level of Sr, compared to $\approx 50\,\mathrm{V/cm}$ for low-$\ell$ states.}
elements.sent-2411-058: In our paper, we focus on levels up to $n = 73$ and, extrapolating from the data above, we limit the considered electric fields to $20\,\mathrm{V/cm}$.
elements.sent-2411-059: We use the $\ket{n,\ell,m_\ell}$ basis to describe the atomic internal levels, where $n$ is the principal quantum number of the valence electron, $\ell$ is its angular orbital momentum, and $m_\ell$ is the projection of $\ell$ on the quantization axis.
elements.sent-2411-060: Circular states are those which have the maximum possible $|m_\ell| = \ell = n-1$.
elements.sent-2411-061: As the spin-orbit splitting is insignificant for circular states, we can ignore the fine structure.
elements.sent-2411-062: For alkali-metal atoms, in a given $n$ manifold, there are two circular states with $m_\ell = \pm \ell$.
elements.sent-2411-063: Throughout this paper, we denote these circular states as $\ket{n\CC_+}$ and $\ket{n\CC_-}$, according to the sign of $m_\ell$.
elements.sent-2411-064: We are also interested in the elliptical states, characterized by $|m_\ell| = n-2$.
elements.sent-2411-065: There are four elliptical states for given $n$ [with $\ell = (n-2)$ or $\ell = (n-1)$].
elements.sent-2411-066: For the sake of demonstration, in this paper we specifically focus on two of them, denoted as $\ket{n \EE_\pm = \ket{n , \ell = (n-2) , m_\ell = \pm\ell}$.}
elements.sent-2411-067: For zero electric and magnetic fields, $\ket{n\CC_\pm}$ and $\ket{n\EE_\pm}$ are eigenstates of $\hat{h ^{(j)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-068: As the fields increase, these eigenstates adiabatically change into field-dressed eigenstates $\ket{n\Cdr_\pm}$ and $\ket{n\Edr_\pm}$, which admix levels with different $n$ and $\ell$ but retain well-defined values of $m_\ell$.
elements.sent-2411-069: We find the compositions and energies of the field-dressed atomic eigenstates by constructing and numerically diagonalizing the one-body Hamiltonian, using procedures implemented in the Python library {\tt pairinteraction ~\cite{2017-Weber-JPB .}}
elements.sent-2411-070: For simplicity, in the two-body interaction terms $\hat{V ^{(j,k)}$ we only consider electric dipole-dipole interactions.}
elements.sent-2411-071: We omit the higher-order multipole-multipole interactions, which are generally weaker than dipole interactions by one or more orders of magnitude~\cite{2021-Cohen-PRXQ .}
elements.sent-2411-072: We denote the electric dipole operator acting on atom $j$ as $\hat{d ^{(j)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-073: Its components $\hat{d ^{(j) _q$ can be written in terms of the spherical coordinate system, where basis vectors are labeled by $q = 0,\pm1$: $\vec{e _0 \equiv \vec{e _Z, \vec{e _{\pm 1 \equiv \mp (\vec{e _X \pm i \vec{e _Y) / \sqrt{2}$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: The dipole-dipole interaction term can then be written succinctly as follows~\cite{2003-Brown-Book : \begin{equation}\label{eq:dipole-dipole-short-form} \hat{V}^{(j,k)} = \sum_{q',q=-1}^{+1} v^{(j,k)}_{q';q} \hat{d}^{(j)}_{q'} \hat{d}^{(k)}_q,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}v^{(j,k)}_{0;0} = 2 v^{(j,k)}_{+1;-1} = 2 v^{(j,k)}_{-1;+1} = \frac{1-3 \cos^2 \theta_{jk}}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 |R_{jk}|^3}, \label{eq:dipole-dipole-angular-factors} v^{(j,k)}_{0;\pm 1} = v^{(j,k)}_{\pm1;0} = \pm \frac{3}{\sqrt{2}} \frac{ \sin\theta_{jk}\cos\theta_{jk} } {4 \pi \epsilon_0 |R_{jk}|^3} e^{\mp i\phi_{jk}}, \nonumber v^{(j,k)}_{\pm 1; \pm 1} = -\frac{3}{2} \frac{ \sin^2\theta_{jk} } {4 \pi \epsilon_0 |R_{jk}|^3} e^{\mp i2\phi_{jk}}. \nonumber\end{equation} Here $\vec{R _{jk = \vec{R _k - \vec{R _j$, $\theta_{jk}$ is the polar angle of $\vec{R _{jk}$ ($\cos \theta_{jk = \vec{R _{jk}\cdot \vec{e _0/|R_{jk |$), and $\phi_{jk}$ is the azimuthal angle of rotation about the $\vec{e _0$ axis (relative to an arbitrarily chosen $X$ axis).}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-075: Per the electric dipole selection rules (${ \Delta \ell = \pm 1, \Delta m_\ell = 0,\pm 1 }$), the dipole operators allow the coupling between neighboring circular levels ${ \ket{n\Cdr_\pm \leftrightarrow \ket{(n+1)\Cdr_\pm }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-076: The corresponding transition dipole moments scale as ${ \sim n^2 e a_0 }$~\cite{1994-Gallagher-Book .}
elements.sent-2411-077: Similarly, the couplings ${ \ket{n\Cdr_\pm \leftrightarrow \ket{n\Edr_\pm }$ and ${ \ket{n\Cdr_\pm \leftrightarrow \ket{(n+2)\Edr_\pm }$ are dipole-allowed, with a matrix element scaling as ${ \sim n^{3/2 e a_0 }$~\cite{1994-Gallagher-Book .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: The coupling ${ \ket{n\Cdr_\pm \leftrightarrow \ket{(n+1)\Edr_\pm }$ becomes dipole-allowed in an electric field, because the field-dressed level ${ \ket{(n+1)\Edr_\pm }$ contains a large admixture of an elliptical state with ${ \ell = n }$; the resulting matrix element likewise scales as ${ \sim n^{3/2 e a_0 }$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: In quantum simulation of $S$-spin particles, a common strategy is to encode the $2S+1$ spin states in a particular subspace of atomic levels.
elements.sent-2411-080: We expand upon this approach by defining two separate subspaces, which correspond to two distinct spin species.
elements.sent-2411-081: If we select two subspaces which significantly differ with regards to transition matrix elements between the constituent levels, the atoms can effectively represent two distinct spin species with characteristic inter- and intra-species interaction strengths.
elements.sent-2411-082: This scheme allows to assign different effective species to identical atoms simply by preparing them in the appropriate subspaces.
elements.sent-2411-083: In the following discussion, we focus on simulating 1/2-spin particles, so that each chosen subspace will consist of two levels.
elements.sent-2411-084: Extending this scheme to higher $S$ may be possible, although likely presents additional complications.
elements.sent-2411-085: For the first spin species, we choose a pair of neighboring circular states $\ket{n\Cdr_\pm}$ and $\ket{(n+1)\Cdr_\pm}$, corresponding to the 1/2-spin states $\ket{\Ua}$ and $\ket{\Da}$ (which can be assigned in either order).
elements.sent-2411-086: We label their field-dressed energies as $\En_\Ua$ and $\En_\Da$.
elements.sent-2411-087: This assignment of states represents the established approach to effective spin simulation, known from previous literature~\cite{2018-Nguyen-PRX,2021-Cohen-PRXQ .}
elements.sent-2411-088: For the second spin species, we choose a different circular state $\ket{n' \Cdr_\pm}$ paired with either $\ket{(n'+1) \Edr_\pm}$ or $\ket{(n'+2) \Edr_\pm}$.
elements.sent-2411-089: The corresponding effective spin states are denoted as $\ket{\ua}$ and $\ket{\da}$ respectively, with energies $\En_\ua$ and $\En_\da$.
elements.sent-2411-090: We refer to these two types of effective spins as the CC species and the CE species, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-091: For brevity, we will use the term "CC atom" to designate an atom excited into the first subspace, and similarly for "CE atom".
elements.sent-2411-092: Spin-exchanging interactions between different species are often a desirable part of the system dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-093: The spin exchange between CC and CE species depends on the F\"orster defect ${\Delta = (\En_\Ua - \En_\Da) - (\En_\ua - \En_\da)}$, which expresses the energy cost of a single exchange.
elements.sent-2411-094: To enable resonant spin exchange, $\Delta$ should be comparable to, or smaller than, the spin exchange matrix element.
elements.sent-2411-095: This can be achieved by tuning the external electric and magnetic fields to minimize $\Delta$.
elements.sent-2411-096: Throughout the rest of this paper, whenever analyzing the system properties in different fields, we consistently adjust $\Edc$ and $B$ together in order to minimize $\Delta$.
elements.sent-2411-097: For given $\Edc$, we define $B_\mathrm{res}$ as the value of $B$ where the F\"orster defect is $\Delta = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-098: In practice, since realistically the ability to control $B$ is limited by environmental field fluctuations and other sources of error, we define $B_\mathrm{res}$ only with an accuracy down to $0.01\,\mathrm{Gauss}$, so $\Delta$ in our calculations has a residual nonzero value.
elements.sent-2411-099: Achieving interspecies spin exchange places constrains on the choice of CC and CE effective spin levels.
elements.sent-2411-100: The values of $n$ and $n'$ should be chosen in such a way that the transition frequencies $\En_\Ua-\En_\Da$ and $\En_\ua-\En_\da$ are similar in zero field, and therefore can be tuned into resonance via small external fields (on the order of $\sim 1\,\mathrm{V/cm}$ and $\sim 100\,\mathrm{Gauss}$).
elements.sent-2411-101: For the scheme $n \rightarrow n+1, n' \rightarrow n'+1$, the values of $n$ and $n'$ should therefore be similar, while for the scheme $n \rightarrow n+1, n' \rightarrow n'+2$, generally one has $n' \gg n$.
elements.sent-2411-102: Additionally, interspecies spin exchange constrains the assignment of the spin level labels: if for species CC we assign the higher-energy state to $\Ua$ and the lower-energy state to $\Da$, then for species CE we need to assign $\ua, \da$ in the same way, so that the pair states $\ket{\Ua \da}$ and $\ket{\Da \ua}$ can have similar total energy.
elements.sent-2411-103: The sign of $m_\ell$ picked for the two species can be the same or different.
elements.sent-2411-104: However, picking level pairs with opposite signs of $m_\ell$ (e.g., ${ \ket{n \Cdr_- ,\ket{(n+1) \Cdr_- }$ and ${ \ket{n' \Cdr_+ , \ket{(n'+2) \Edr_+ }$) helps ensure that the Zeeman shifts of the transition frequencies have different signs, which makes it easier to match the transition frequencies.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-105: Additionally, the extreme difference in $m_\ell$ helps to prevent mixing between the two spin state subspaces.
elements.sent-2411-106: It is worth noting that the electric field $\vecEdc$, acting along the $Z$-axis, has a different effect on circular and elliptical states~\cite{1994-Gallagher-Book .}
elements.sent-2411-107: Specifically, circular states lack a first-order Stark shift, which is normally caused by mixing to nearby levels with the same $m_\ell$ and opposite symmetry; a circular state $\ket{n \CC}$ has no partners with the same $m_\ell$ within the same $n$ manifold.
elements.sent-2411-108: For this reason, in the relatively small fields $\Edc \sim 1\,\mathrm{V/cm}$ that we consider, the energy and composition of $\ket{n \Cdr_\pm}$ are only negligibly affected by $\Edc$ (only a weak second-order Stark effect occurs).
elements.sent-2411-109: On the other hand, the elliptical states $\ket{n \Edr_\pm}$ display a much stronger first-order Stark shift.
elements.sent-2411-110: This will be shown to be particularly relevant for the effective spin interactions and their tunability via electric fields.
elements.sent-2411-111: Finally, we emphasize that a two-species model with inter-species spin exchange cannot be easily realized using only circular states, i.e., using the subspaces $\ket{n \CC \rightarrow \ket{(n+1) \CC}$ and $\ket{n' \CC \rightarrow \ket{(n'+1) \CC}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-112: In this approach, to make the two species meaningfully different, it is necessary to use highly different $n, n'$ (to obtain different interaction strengths).

elements.sent-2411-259: Integrating both spin species in a single simulated model provides significant benefits over a single-species setup, both for spin models and lattice models.
elements.sent-2411-260: First, a two-species approach offers greater flexibility in designing interactions between different parts of the system.
elements.sent-2411-261: For example, it allows to easily divide a simulated lattice model into two sub-lattices with independent properties.
elements.sent-2411-262: Second, the broader range of "magic angles" accessible with the two-species setup gives more flexibility in designing the geometry of the simulator.
elements.sent-2411-263: For example, a spin chain with suppressed transverse interactions can be achieved using a line of CC spins set at the specific angle $\theta = 0.304\pi$, but it can also be realized with CE spins set at almost any polar angle, enabling new designs for the simulation array.
elements.sent-2411-264: Third, the CE spins allow more precise tuning of Ising-like interactions, which for CC spins are restricted to a narrow range near $\theta = 0.304\pi$ and are highly sensitive to atom positioning.
elements.sent-2411-265: These combined advantages make the dual-species approach more robust and versatile.
elements.sent-2411-266: To illustrate the potential applications of our setup, we describe two examples of realizable system geometries.
elements.sent-2411-267: These examples will also demonstrate the advantages of combining the CC and CE spins, compared to CC spins alone.

When the quantization axis (axis $Z$) is oriented at an angle $\pi/4$ relative to the unit cell sides, the interactions between neighboring atoms within each lattice, shown as blue solid lines (lattice ``A'') and red long-dashed lines (lattice ``B''), become uniform. However, the inter-lattice interactions (green short-dashed lines) display spatial anisotropy, only acting along one direction.} \label{fig:two-species-example_systems-figure_1}

elements.sent-2411-297: The implementation of the setup relies on the ability to trap individual Rydberg atoms at specific positions.
elements.sent-2411-298: In experiments using ultracold alkali-metal atoms in their ground states, red-detuned optical tweezers are typically used to trap the atoms at desired locations.
elements.sent-2411-299: However, these tweezers become repulsive for atoms excited to Rydberg states, due to the strong ponderomotive potential of the Rydberg electron~\cite{2000-Dutta-PRL .}
elements.sent-2411-300: Consequently, experiments involving Rydberg atoms require alternative trapping methods.
elements.sent-2411-301: Currently, the standard method of optically trapping Rydberg atoms uses "bottle-beam" hollow traps.
elements.sent-2411-302: Atoms are confined in an intensity minimum at the center of a laser beam, which is created using a spatial light modulator~\cite{2020-Barredo-PRL .}
elements.sent-2411-303: In a recent experiment, this technique was successfully used to create an array of individually trapped rubidium atoms in circular states~\cite{2023-Ravon-PRL .}
elements.sent-2411-304: For alkaline-earth atoms, other trapping methods are possible.
elements.sent-2411-305: The ionic core of the alkaline-earth atom remains optically active even in Rydberg states, due to the second valence electron remaining close to the core.
elements.sent-2411-306: This allows to trap circular-state atoms in conventional optical tweezers, as the ionic core's interaction with the tweezer overpowers the contribution from the ponderomotive potential of the Rydberg electron.
elements.sent-2411-307: Tweezer trapping has been experimentally demonstrated for low-$\ell$ Yb Rydberg states~\cite{2022-Wilson-PRL and, very recently, for circular states of Sr~\cite{2024-Holzl-PRX .}}
elements.sent-2411-308: The trapping of elliptical states has received comparatively less attention from the scientific community.
elements.sent-2411-309: To date, to the best of our knowledge, there have been no explicit demonstrations of trapping few-atom systems in elliptical states.
elements.sent-2411-310: However, it is reasonable to anticipate that methods developed for circular state trapping can be adapted to elliptical-state atoms.
elements.subsection-2411-008: Influence of motional degrees of freedom
elements.sent-2411-311: For simplicity, we treat the atoms as fixed points throughout this paper.
elements.sent-2411-312: However, atomic motion within the traps can significantly impact the system's dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-313: For traps with low frequency $\omega$, dipole-dipole interactions between atoms can be strong enough to excite higher motional states.
elements.sent-2411-314: The resulting fluctuations in $\vec{R_i -\vec{R_j}$ can lead to entanglement between the atomic motion and the internal-level dynamics, equivalent to spin-motion coupling, which induces unwanted dephasing effects.}
elements.sent-2411-315: This interaction-induced motion coupling has been experimentally demonstrated for circular-state atoms in Ref.~\cite{2024-Mehaignerie-Arxiv .}
elements.sent-2411-316: Thermal excitations of atomic motion can cause similar issues.
elements.sent-2411-317: Ref.~\cite{2023-Mehaignerie-PRA observed significant positional oscillations (up to $1\,\mathrm{\mu m}$) in a setup with two circular-state atoms under typical conditions (trap frequency $\omega=22.8,\mathrm{kHz}$; interaction strength $\sim 1,\mathrm{MHz}$ at a distance of $14,\mathrm{\mu m}$; room temperature).}
elements.sent-2411-318: To mitigate these dephasing effects during long-time evolution, our setup should operate at cryogenic temperatures (a few $\mathrm{\mu K}$ or lower), and trap frequencies should be as high as possible.
elements.sent-2411-319: Carrying out simulations of specific scenarios requires not only preparing atoms in precisely fixed positions, but also preparing each individual atom in a desired circular or elliptical state, rapidly and reliably.
elements.sent-2411-320: A number of methods exist that allow rapid excitation of circular levels.
elements.sent-2411-321: One well-established approach is the adiabatic rapid-passage method~\cite{1983-Hulet-PRL,2020-Cortinas-PRL .}
elements.sent-2411-322: The atoms, driven into low-$|m_\ell|$ states, are subjected to a radio-frequency ac field, combined with parallel magnetic and electric fields.
elements.sent-2411-323: Under the combined effects of these fields, the atomic spectrum exhibits an avoided crossing, connecting the low-$|m_\ell|$ state to a circular state of the same $n$.
elements.sent-2411-324: This crossing can then be traversed by adiabatically tuning the electric field.
elements.sent-2411-325: A faster variant of the adiabatic rapid-passage method has been proposed as well~\cite{2017-Signoles-PRL .}
elements.sent-2411-326: This variant exploits the fact that, for higher $\ell$, transitions between states with successive $\ell$ are all of nearly the same frequency.
elements.sent-2411-327: In the procedure, the electric field is kept constant, so that this transition frequency is on resonance with the chosen radio-frequency radiation.
elements.sent-2411-328: A radio-frequency pulse is then applied for a precise time, driving Rabi oscillations between the initial state (low $|m_\ell|$) and the circular state.
elements.sent-2411-329: After a $\pi$ pulse, the population is coherently transferred into the circular state.
elements.sent-2411-330: Another well-established approach is the crossed-fields method.
elements.sent-2411-331: Here the atoms are initially placed in perpendicularly crossing electric and magnetic fields, and excited into $m_\ell = 0$ states of the desired $n$.
elements.sent-2411-332: The electric field is then adiabatically switched off, while the transverse magnetic field remains in place.
elements.sent-2411-333: This process transfers the atoms into circular states $\ket{n \CC}$.
elements.sent-2411-334: Note, however, that in our setup this method poses complications: once the circular state preparation is finished, it is still necessary to turn on an electric field parallel to the magnetic field, which needs to be carefully done to avoid disturbing the circular excitations~\cite{1989-Cheret-EPL,1997-Lutwak-PRA,2018-Morgan-PRA .}
elements.sent-2411-335: Other fast circularization methods involve using quantum control theory, i.e. designing specially-shaped radio-frequency pulses that circularize the atomic array with optimal speed and fidelity~\cite{2018-Patsch-PRA,2020-Larrouy-PRX .}
elements.sent-2411-336: The excitation of elliptical states, which are a key aspect of our setup, is a less developed topic compared to circular state excitation.
elements.sent-2411-338: A straightforward method is to produce atoms in $\ket{(n-1) \CC}$ states, then transfer them to $\ket{n \EE}$ states via a $\pi$-polarized microwave transition.
elements.sent-2411-339: It is also likely that existing circularization schemes can be adapted to produce elliptical states.
elements.sent-2411-340: For example, $\ket{n \EE}$ states can be produced with a high fidelity via optimally shaped pulses~\cite{2024-Huls-Personal .}
elements.sent-2411-341: Another possibility is a proposed "quantum Zeno effect" scheme~\cite{2014-Signoles-NatPhys .}
elements.sent-2411-342: The atom is prepared in a $\ket{n \CC}$ state, then a radio-frequency field is applied with a frequency equal to the transition between the different high-$\ell$ levels $\ket{n, \ell}$.
elements.sent-2411-343: This makes the atom state evolve back and forth through the entire ladder of Rydberg levels.
elements.sent-2411-344: By applying a microwave transition to couple a level $\ket{n,\ell'}$ to $\ket{n-1,\ell'}$, splitting that level into two, the atom state is "blocked" from evolving past $\ket{n,\ell'}$.
elements.sent-2411-345: In this way, one could prepare the elliptical state for given $n$, or even a linear combination of circular and elliptical states.
elements.sent-2411-346: Another key requirement of our proposal is the ability to address individual atoms in the setup, in order to initialize each atom into the desired state.
elements.sent-2411-347: Most methods of circular state preparation rely on radio-frequency radiation, which has a large wavelength (on the order of milimeters or above) and thus cannot be locally addressed in systems of Rydberg atoms, where interparticle distances are measured in microns.
elements.sent-2411-348: Several proposed approaches address this difficulty by using optical-frequency beams that are addressable on the micron scale.
elements.sent-2411-349: The proposals in Refs.~\cite{2021-Cohen-PRXQ, 2020-Cardman-PRA use superpositions of copropagating Laguerre-Gauss modes, generating rotating intensity patterns that can drive transitions with tunable frequency and angular momentum transfer (dependent on the properties of the chosen modes).}
elements.sent-2411-350: This enables the excitation of individual atoms from low-$\ell$ Rydberg states to circular states.
elements.sent-2411-351: Another approach~\cite{2014-Labuhn-PRA uses optical-frequency addressing beams during the initial Rydberg excitation, to temporarily make specific atoms off-resonant with the excitation beam.}
elements.sent-2411-352: Another possibility opens for alkaline-earth Rydberg atoms.
elements.sent-2411-353: In these atoms, the circular state of the valence electron experiences an energy shift when the ionic atom core is optically excited.
elements.sent-2411-354: This allows optical-wavelength, site-resolved control of the circular states~\cite{2022-Muni-NatPhys,2024-Wirth-PRL .}
elements.sent-2411-355: Other approaches leverage ancilla atoms to utilize the Rydberg blockade effect, where interactions between two Rydberg atoms shift the energy levels, making simultaneous excitation off-resonant and preventing the excitation of both atoms at once.
elements.sent-2411-356: For example, Ref.~\cite{2023-Mehaignerie-Thesis describes a simple scheme where a two-atom $\ket{\Uparrow \Downarrow }$ state is prepared by using a third ancilla atom.}
elements.sent-2411-357: Depending on its position, the ancilla induces an angle-dependent Rydberg blockade interaction with a nearby atom, which thus becomes off-resonant with the excitation field.
elements.sent-2411-358: By moving the ancilla, the two atoms can therefore be excited into the desired states one at a time.
elements.sent-2411-359: For certain system geometries, it is possible to use more specialized methods of initial state preparation.
elements.sent-2411-360: For example, in a spin chain of regularly-spaced atoms, one can use optical tweezers to temporarily displace the neighbors of the atom of interest.
elements.sent-2411-361: This shifts the resonant frequencies of the atom, and its state can then be controlled individually~\cite{2016-Nguyen-Thesis .}
elements.sent-2411-362: Tracking the simulator state requires state detection on the level of individual atoms, with the ability to distinguish different circular and elliptical states.
elements.sent-2411-363: Optical detection allows to resolve the presence of atoms on individual sites, but in general it does not allow discerning different internal levels.
elements.sent-2411-364: It can, however, be combined with other methods, which rely on removing atoms of specific levels from the array, followed by optical detection to find out which atoms are now missing.
elements.sent-2411-365: This allows a simple way of site-resolved state detection ~\cite{2023-Ravon-PRL .}
elements.sent-2411-366: A frequent approach to many-body state readout is the field ionization method: the electric field is gradually increased to cross the ionization thresholds of successive atomic levels.
elements.sent-2411-367: By counting the emitted ions as a function of the ionizing field, and matching specific atomic states to their predicted threshold fields, populations of individual states can be measured.
elements.sent-2411-368: This method is widely applied experimentally to detect circular Rydberg states~\cite{1983-Hulet-PRL,1986-Liang-PRA,2020-Teixeira-PRL,2020-CantatMoltrecht-PRR .}
elements.sent-2411-369: By itself, the ionization method allows to measure the overall population, but not to detect the states on selected sites.
elements.sent-2411-370: This limitation can be overcome, e.g., by selectively ionizing atoms in one of the states, then imaging the atomic array to identify which atoms were ionized ~\cite{2015-Barredo-PRL,2023-Ravon-PRL .}
elements.sent-2411-371: Another limitation of the ionization method is that it generally cannot distinguish $\ket{n \CC}$ and $\ket{n \EE}$ states, which have very similar threshold ionization fields ~\cite{2020-CantatMoltrecht-PRR .}
elements.sent-2411-372: However, this issue can also be overcome, e.g., by first transferring the elliptical-state atoms to a level from a different energy manifold ~\cite{2014-Signoles-NatPhys,2018-Dietsche-Thesis .}
elements.sent-2411-373: Other atom-resolved state detection methods are also possible.
elements.sent-2411-374: For instance, non-destructive state measurements can use ancilla atoms that become resonant or non-resonant with a driving field, depending on the state of a nearby array atom~\cite{2021-Cohen-PRXQ .}
elements.sent-2411-375: Specialized methods of atom-resolved state detection can also be applied in specific configurations.
elements.sent-2411-376: For example, Ref.~\cite{2016-Nguyen-Thesis considers a one-dimensional spin chain trapped in a lattice, bordered on both ends by ``plug'' beams.}
elements.sent-2411-377: To detect the state of the chain atom-by-atom, the lattice is switched off and the plug beam is slowly reduced, expelling atoms one by one from the right-hand end of the chain.
elements.sent-2411-378: This allows to individually read out the atomic states, e.g., by field ionization.
elements.sent-2411-379: For alkaline-earth Rydberg atoms, another detection method is available, which makes use of the electrostatic coupling between the two valence electrons.
elements.sent-2411-380: The ionic core electron can be driven into a fluorescing state, whose excitation probability depends on the state of the Rydberg electron.
elements.sent-2411-381: By measuring the fluorescence across the system, it is then possible to measure the Rydberg electron state in a quantum-nondestructive way~\cite{2022-Muni-NatPhys .}
elements.sent-2411-382: Radiative lifetimes of Rydberg atoms are typically the dominant limitation of the simulation time.
elements.sent-2411-383: Long radiative lifetimes are the largest advantage of using circular states.
elements.sent-2411-384: Circular states have only one dipole-allowed pathway for spontaneous emission: the {$\sigma$-polarized photon transition $\ket{n \CC \rightarrow \ket{(n-1) \CC}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-385: Neglecting the blackbody radiation, the circular state radiative lifetime scales as $n^5$ (typically being on order of $10^{-2}\,\mathrm{s}$ in cryogenic environments), compared to the $n^4$ scaling for low-$\ell$ Rydberg states (lifetime on order of $10^{-3}\,\mathrm{s}$)~\cite{2010-Saffman-RevModPhys,2022-Kruckenhauser-QST .}
elements.sent-2411-386: For an $N$-atom system, the overall system lifetime, limited by spontaneous emission, is therefore on the order of $10^{-2 /N\,\mathrm{s}$.}
elements.sent-2411-387: The lifetime should be considered in light of the spin-exchange time scale $1/C^{jk _{+-}$.}
elements.sent-2411-388: Usually, throughout the simulation duration, one would want to simulate at least $10^2$ spin-exchange periods.
elements.sent-2411-389: Assuming that $C^{jk _{+-}$ are generally on the order of $10^5\,\mathrm{Hz}$ (as is the case in the example system shown in Section~\ref{sec:effective-interactions ), this means that for $N\sim 10$ systems the circular state lifetime is sufficient for at least $10^2$ spin-exchange periods.}}
elements.sent-2411-390: The circular state lifetime can be further extended by placing atoms in a small cavity (on the order of a few millimeters) which does not support photon modes with a wavelength corresponding to the spontaneous emission.
elements.sent-2411-391: Theoretical calculations show that such cavities can extend the radiative lifetime of circular atoms to times $\sim 10^1\,\mathrm{s}$~\cite{2018-Nguyen-PRX,2021-Cohen-PRXQ .}
elements.sent-2411-392: A crude ``cavity'', realized by placing the atoms between two capacitor plates, is sufficient to suppress the $\sigma$-polarized spontaneous emission for circular states~\cite{2018-Nguyen-PRX .}
elements.sent-2411-393: This apparatus does not help for elliptical states, because they have additional emission pathways, including $\pi$-polarized photon emissions.
elements.sent-2411-394: However, cavities of more sophisticated shapes can suppress also $\pi$-polarized photon emission, extending the lifetime of elliptical states as well~\cite{2021-Cohen-PRXQ .}
elements.sent-2411-395: Real experiments also need to contend with other sources of decoherence, such as noise in the external fields, collisions with residual background gas, etc.~\cite{2018-Nguyen-PRX .}
elements.sent-2411-396: As an example from practice, in the experiment described in Ref.~\cite{2020-CantatMoltrecht-PRR , circular $n=50$ Rb atoms were prepared at $10\,\mathrm{\mu K}$ temperature.}
elements.sent-2411-397: The measured radiative lifetime was $3.7\times10^{-3}\,\mathrm{s}$ (without an inhibiting cavity); however, additional factors, such as electric field noise and fluctuations of the magnetic field, lowering the overall timescale of irreversible decoherence to $2.7\times10^{-4}\,\mathrm{s}$.
elements.sent-2411-398: Therefore, the magnitude of these factors needs to be calculated separately, depending on the specific experimental approach.
elements.sent-2411-399: Another factor is the stimulated black-body absorption and emission that occurs at nonzero temperatures.
elements.sent-2411-400: While recent experiments have achieved radiative lifetimes of up to $10^{-3 ,\mathrm{s}$ for circular-state atoms at room temperature~\cite{2020-CantatMoltrecht-PRR,2023-Wu-PRL,2024-Holzl-PRX , minimizing these effects is desirable.}}
elements.sent-2411-401: This, along with the suppression of motional excitations, further motivates the use of cryogenic temperatures in experiments.
elements.sent-2411-402: To maintain stable values of interaction coefficients and F\"orster defects over time, precise control of stray electric and magnetic fields is required.
elements.sent-2411-403: For the parameter sets used in our previous examples, we have numerically checked that maintaining the F\"orster defect below $50\,\mathrm{kHz}$ requires stabilizing the electric field to within $10^{-3}\,\mathrm{V/cm}$, and the magnetic field to within $10^{-2}$ Gauss.
elements.sent-2411-404: These accuracy levels are achievable in experimental settings.
elements.sent-2411-405: Electric fields can be measured and compensated with an accuracy of approximately $10^{-5}\,\mathrm{V/cm}$~\cite{1999-Osterwalder-PRL,2014-Huber-NatComm , while magnetic fields can be controlled with high precision, even down to about $10^{-5}$ Gauss~\cite{2023-Borkowski-RevSciIns,2024-Rogora-PRA .}}
elements.sent-2411-406: Hence this level of field stability is experimentally feasible.
elements.sent-2411-407: In this paper, we have presented a comparative analysis of two methods for simulating spin-1/2 particles using Rydberg states.
elements.sent-2411-408: The first method uses two circular states to represent the spin states, as used in previous proposals, while the second introduces a novel scheme by encoding the spin states in a circular state and an elliptical state.
elements.sent-2411-409: These two approaches result in two distinct "spin species" (named "CC" and "CE" respectively) with different intra- and inter-species spin interactions.
elements.sent-2411-410: We focused on one scheme, where the CE spin is simulated by two states with principal quantum numbers $n',n'+2$; we found that the CC-CC and CC-CE interactions are typically Heisenberg-like, with strong transverse couplings, while CE-CE interactions are more Ising-like, dominated by longitudinal couplings.
elements.sent-2411-411: Combining these two approaches in one setup allows to simulate a variety of two-species spin models, or lattice systems with two sublattices.
elements.sent-2411-412: We have illustrated the potential applications by providing specific examples of models that could be realized using this framework.
elements.sent-2411-413: This includes: a lattice system with anisotropic interactions between sublattices, and a pair of coupled Su-Schrieffer-Heeger chains, which have applications for studying topological models.
elements.sent-2411-414: The setup is experimentally feasible in current ultracold physics laboratories, potentially extending quantum simulations to larger 2D or 3D systems that are difficult to model with traditional numerical methods.
elements.sent-2411-415: While the incorporation of elliptical states extends the possibilities of spin simulation, it also presents new challenges.
elements.sent-2411-416: The techniques for creating and trapping elliptical Rydberg atoms are currently less developed, compared to those for circular states.
elements.sent-2411-417: Additionally, the current scheme, with large energy differences between pseudo-spin states ($\En_\Ua - \En_\Da$ and $\En_\ua - \En_\da$), is not easily used for qubit applications due to the rapid dephasing of imprinted phase differences.
